**Abergavenny** ([/ˌæbərɡəˈvɛni/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Y Fenni*, pronounced [[ə ˈvɛni]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Welsh "Help:IPA/Welsh"), archaically *Abergafenni*, 'mouth of the [River Gavenny](/wiki/River_Gavenny "River Gavenny")') is a [market town](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") and [community](/wiki/Community_(Wales) "Community (Wales)") in [Monmouthshire](/wiki/Monmouthshire "Monmouthshire"), [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"). Abergavenny is promoted as a "Gateway to Wales"; it is approximately 7 miles (11 km) from the [border with England](/wiki/England%E2%80%93Wales_border "England–Wales border") ([Herefordshire](/wiki/Herefordshire "Herefordshire")) and is located where the [A40 trunk road](/wiki/A40_road "A40 road") and the recently upgraded [A465 Heads of the Valleys road](/wiki/A465_road "A465 road") meet.[[2]](#cite_note-BBC-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Frommers-3)

Originally the site of a [Roman fort](/wiki/Castra "Castra"), [Gobannium](/wiki/Gobannium "Gobannium"), it became a [medieval](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") [walled town](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall") within the [Welsh Marches](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches"). The town contains the remains of a medieval stone [castle](/wiki/Castle "Castle") built soon after the [Norman conquest of Wales](/wiki/Norman_invasion_of_Wales "Norman invasion of Wales").

Abergavenny is situated at the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the [River Usk](/wiki/River_Usk "River Usk") and a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary "Tributary") stream, the Gavenny.[[4]](#cite_note-EB-4) It is almost entirely surrounded by mountains and hills: the [Blorenge](/wiki/Blorenge "Blorenge") (559 m, 1,834 ft),[[5]](#cite_note-5) the [Sugar Loaf](/wiki/Sugar_Loaf,_Monmouthshire "Sugar Loaf, Monmouthshire") (596 m, 1,955 ft), [Skirrid Fawr](/wiki/Skirrid_Fawr "Skirrid Fawr") (Great Skirrid), [Ysgyryd Fach](/wiki/Ysgyryd_Fach "Ysgyryd Fach") (Little Skirrid), Deri, Rholben and Mynydd Llanwenarth, known locally as "[Llanwenarth](/wiki/Llanwenarth "Llanwenarth") Breast". Abergavenny provides access to the nearby [Black Mountains](/wiki/Black_Mountains,_Wales "Black Mountains, Wales") and the wider [Brecon Beacons National Park](/wiki/Brecon_Beacons "Brecon Beacons"). The [Cambrian Way](/wiki/Cambrian_Way "Cambrian Way"), [Beacons Way](/wiki/Beacons_Way "Beacons Way") and [Marches Way](/wiki/Marches_Way "Marches Way") pass through Abergavenny whilst the [Offa's Dyke Path](/wiki/Offa%27s_Dyke_Path "Offa's Dyke Path") passes through [Pandy](/wiki/Pandy,_Monmouthshire "Pandy, Monmouthshire") five miles to the north and the [Usk Valley Walk](/wiki/Usk_Valley_Walk "Usk Valley Walk") passes through nearby [Llanfoist](/wiki/Llanfoist "Llanfoist").

In the UK 2011 census, the six relevant wards (Lansdown, Grofield, [Castle](/wiki/Castle_(Abergavenny_ward) "Castle (Abergavenny ward)"), Croesonen, Cantref and [Priory](/wiki/Priory_(Abergavenny_ward) "Priory (Abergavenny ward)")) collectively listed Abergavenny's population as 12,515. The town hosted the 2016 [National Eisteddfod of Wales](/wiki/National_Eisteddfod_of_Wales "National Eisteddfod of Wales").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The town derives its name from a [Brythonic](/wiki/Brythonic_languages "Brythonic languages") word *Gobannia* meaning "river of the blacksmiths", and relates to the town's pre-Roman importance in iron [smelting](/wiki/Smelting "Smelting"). The name is related to the modern Welsh word *gof* ([blacksmith](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith")), and so is also associated with the Welsh smith [Gofannon](/wiki/Gofannon "Gofannon") from folklore. The river later became, in Welsh, *Gafenni*, and the town's name became *Abergafenni*, meaning "mouth of (Welsh: *Aber*) the Gavenny (*Gafenni*)". In Welsh, the shortened form *Y Fenni* may have come into use after about the 15th century, and is now used as the Welsh name. Abergavenny, the English spelling, is in general use.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

The town originally developed on the high ground to the north of the [floodplain](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain") of the River Usk and to the west of the valley of the much smaller Gavenny River though has since extended to the east of the latter. It has merged with the originally separate settlement of Mardy to the north but remains separate from that of Llanfoist to the south due to the presence of the river and its floodplain; nevertheless Llanfoist is in many ways a suburb of the town. The ground rises gradually in the north of the town before steepening to form the Deri and Rholben spurs of Sugar Loaf. The A4143 crossing of the Usk by means of the historic [Usk Bridge](/wiki/Abergavenny_Bridge "Abergavenny Bridge") is sited at the narrowest point of the floodplain, a site also chosen for the former crossing of a tramroad and the later mainline railway. The high ground at either side is formed by a legacy of the last ice age, the [recessional Llanfoist moraine](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine") which underlies both the village which gives it its name, the town centre and the [Nevill Hall area](/wiki/Nevill_Hall_Hospital "Nevill Hall Hospital"). The older parts of the town north of its centre are built upon a relatively flat-lying [alluvial fan](/wiki/Alluvial_fan "Alluvial fan") extending west from the area of St Mary's Priory to Cantref and of similar age to the moraine.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the UK 2011 census, the six relevant wards (Lansdown, Grofield, [Castle](/wiki/Castle_(Abergavenny_ward) "Castle (Abergavenny ward)"), Croesonen, Cantref and [Priory](/wiki/Priory_(Abergavenny_ward) "Priory (Abergavenny ward)")) collectively listed Abergavenny's population as 12,515.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roman period")]

Gobannium was a Roman fort guarding the road along the valley of the River Usk,[[4]](#cite_note-EB-4) which linked the legionary fortress of [Burrium](/wiki/Burrium "Burrium") ([Usk](/wiki/Usk "Usk")) and later [Isca Augusta](/wiki/Isca_Augusta "Isca Augusta") or Isca Silurum ([Caerleon](/wiki/Caerleon "Caerleon")) in the south with [Y Gaer, Brecon](/wiki/Y_Gaer,_Brecon "Y Gaer, Brecon") and [Mid Wales](/wiki/Mid_Wales "Mid Wales"). It was also built to keep the peace among the local [British Iron Age](/wiki/British_Iron_Age "British Iron Age") tribe, the [Silures](/wiki/Silures "Silures").[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Cadw](/wiki/Cadw "Cadw") considers that the fort was occupied from around CE50 to CE150.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Remains of the walls of this fort were discovered west of the castle when excavating the foundations for a new [post office](/wiki/Post_office "Post office") and [telephone exchange](/wiki/Telephone_exchange "Telephone exchange") building in the late 1960s.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### 11th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 11th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/St_Mary%27s_Priory_Church%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1328170.jpg/250px-St_Mary%27s_Priory_Church%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1328170.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_Priory_Church,_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1328170.jpg)

St Mary's Priory Church

Abergavenny grew as a town in early [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") times under the protection of the [Baron Bergavenny (or Abergavenny)](/wiki/Baron_Bergavenny "Baron Bergavenny"). The first Baron was [Hamelin de Balun](/wiki/Hamelin_de_Balun "Hamelin de Balun"), from [Ballon](/wiki/Ballon,_Sarthe "Ballon, Sarthe"), a small town with a castle in Maine near [Le Mans](/wiki/Le_Mans "Le Mans"). Today it is in the [Sarthe département](/wiki/Sarthe_(d%C3%A9partement) "Sarthe (département)") of France. He founded the [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") [priory](/wiki/Priory "Priory"), now the [Priory Church of St Mary](/wiki/Priory_Church_of_St_Mary,_Abergavenny "Priory Church of St Mary, Abergavenny"), in the late 11th century. The Priory belonged originally to the [Benedictine](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") foundation of St. Vincent Abbaye at Le Mans. It was subsequently endowed by [William de Braose](/wiki/William_de_Braose,_4th_Lord_of_Bramber "William de Braose, 4th Lord of Bramber"), with a [tithe](/wiki/Tithe "Tithe") of the profits of the castle and town.[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17) The church contains some unique [alabaster](/wiki/Alabaster "Alabaster") [effigies](/wiki/Effigy "Effigy"), [church monuments](/wiki/Church_monument "Church monument") and unique medieval wood carving, such as the [Tree of Jesse](/wiki/Tree_of_Jesse "Tree of Jesse").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### 12th and 13th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 12th and 13th centuries")]

Owing to its geographical location, the town was frequently embroiled in the border warfare and power play of the 12th and 13th centuries in the Welsh Marches. In 1175, [Abergavenny Castle](/wiki/Abergavenny_Castle "Abergavenny Castle") was the site of a massacre of [Seisyll ap Dyfnwal](/wiki/Seisyll_ap_Dyfnwal "Seisyll ap Dyfnwal") and his associates by [William de Braose, 4th Lord of Bramber](/wiki/William_de_Braose,_4th_Lord_of_Bramber "William de Braose, 4th Lord of Bramber").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Reference to a market at Abergavenny is found in a charter granted to the Prior by William de Braose.[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17)

### 15th to 17th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 15th to 17th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Abergavenny_and_Holy_Mountain%2C_Wales%2C_1890-1900.jpg/250px-Abergavenny_and_Holy_Mountain%2C_Wales%2C_1890-1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abergavenny_and_Holy_Mountain,_Wales,_1890-1900.jpg)

Abergavenny and [Ysgyryd Fawr](/wiki/Ysgyryd_Fawr "Ysgyryd Fawr") at right in the 1890s

[Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr") attacked Abergavenny in 1404. According to popular legend, his raiders gained access to the walled town with the aid of a local woman who sympathised with the rebellion, letting a small party in via the Market Street gate at midnight. They were able to open the gate and allow a much larger party who set fire to the town and plundered its churches and homes leaving Abergavenny Castle intact. Market Street has been referred to as "Traitors' Lane" thereafter. In 1404 Abergavenny was declared its own nation by [Ieuan ab Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Ieuan_ab_Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Ieuan ab Owain Glyndŵr"), illegitimate son of [Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr"). The arrangement lasted approximately two weeks.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

At the [Dissolution of the Monasteries](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Monasteries "Dissolution of the Monasteries") in 1541, the priory's endowment went towards the foundation of a free [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school"), [King Henry VIII Grammar School](/wiki/King_Henry_VIII_Grammar_School "King Henry VIII Grammar School"), the site itself passing to the Gunter family.[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17) During the [Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), prior to the siege of [Raglan Castle](/wiki/Raglan_Castle "Raglan Castle") in 1645, [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") visited Abergavenny and presided in person over the trial of [Sir Trefor Williams, 1st Baronet](/wiki/Sir_Trevor_Williams,_1st_Baronet "Sir Trevor Williams, 1st Baronet") of [Llangibby](/wiki/Llangybi,_Monmouthshire "Llangybi, Monmouthshire"), a [Royalist](/wiki/Cavalier "Cavalier") who changed sides, and other [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead").[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17) In 1639, Abergavenny received a [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") of incorporation under the title of [bailiff](/wiki/Bailiff "Bailiff") and [burgesses](/wiki/Burgess_(word) "Burgess (word)"). A charter with extended privileges was drafted in 1657, but appears never to have been enrolled or to have come into effect. Owing to the refusal of the chief officers of the corporation to take the [oath of allegiance](/wiki/Oath_of_allegiance "Oath of allegiance") to [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") in 1688, the charter was annulled, and the town subsequently declined in prosperity. Chapter 28 of the [1535 Act of Henry VIII](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535%E2%80%931542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535–1542"), which provided that Monmouth, as [county town](/wiki/County_town "County town"), should return one burgess to Parliament, further stated that other [ancient Monmouthshire boroughs](/wiki/Monmouthshire_(historic) "Monmouthshire (historic)") were to contribute towards the payment of the member. In consequence of this clause Abergavenny on various occasions shared in the election, the last instance being in 1685.[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17)

The right to hold two weekly markets and three yearly [fairs](/wiki/Fair "Fair"), beginning in the 13th century, was held ever since as confirmed in 1657.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Abergavenny was celebrated for the production of Welsh [flannel](/wiki/Flannel "Flannel"), and also for the manufacture, whilst the fashion prevailed, of goats' hair [periwigs](/wiki/Periwig "Periwig").[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17)

### 19th and 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 19th and 20th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Abergavenny_boys_national_school_%283374847%29.jpg/250px-Abergavenny_boys_national_school_%283374847%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abergavenny_boys_national_school_(3374847).jpg)

Abergavenny Boys National School (1865)

[Abergavenny railway station](/wiki/Abergavenny_railway_station "Abergavenny railway station"), situated southeast of the town centre, opened on 2 January 1854 as part of the [Newport, Abergavenny and Hereford Railway](/wiki/Newport,_Abergavenny_and_Hereford_Railway "Newport, Abergavenny and Hereford Railway"). The [London North Western Railway](/wiki/London_North_Western_Railway "London North Western Railway") sponsored the construction of the railway linking [Newport station](/wiki/Newport_railway_station "Newport railway station") to [Hereford station](/wiki/Hereford_railway_station "Hereford railway station"). The line was taken over by the [West Midland Railway](/wiki/West_Midland_Railway "West Midland Railway") in 1860 before becoming part of the [Great Western Railway](/wiki/Great_Western_Railway "Great Western Railway") in 1863. A railway line also ran up the valley towards [Brynmawr](/wiki/Brynmawr "Brynmawr") and to [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil "Merthyr Tydfil"); this was closed during the [Beeching cuts](/wiki/Beeching_cuts "Beeching cuts") in the 1960s and the line to [Clydach Gorge](/wiki/Clydach_Gorge "Clydach Gorge") is now a cycle track and footpath. The [Baker Street drill hall](/wiki/Baker_Street_drill_hall,_Abergavenny "Baker Street drill hall, Abergavenny") was completed in 1896.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s deputy, [Rudolf Hess](/wiki/Rudolf_Hess "Rudolf Hess"), was kept under escort at [Maindiff Court Hospital](/wiki/Maindiff_Court_Hospital "Maindiff Court Hospital") during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), after his flight to Britain.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 1964, the [Royal Observer Corps](/wiki/Royal_Observer_Corps "Royal Observer Corps") opened a small monitoring bunker to be used in the event of a nuclear attack. It was closed in 1968 but reopened in 1973 due to the closure of a bunker near [Brynmawr](/wiki/Brynmawr "Brynmawr"). It closed in 1991 on the stand down of the ROC. It remains mostly intact.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Baron of Abergavenny

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Baron of Abergavenny")]

The title of [Baron Abergavenny](/wiki/Baron_Bergavenny "Baron Bergavenny") was first held by the Beauchamp family. In the late 14th century the [reversion](/wiki/Reversion_(law) "Reversion (law)") of the feudal marcher barony (with the castle, town and surrounding lands appurtenant) was purchased from John Hastings, 3rd Earl of Pembroke, who had no heirs, by William Beauchamp, the second son of the Earl of Warwick; and he was elevated to the House of Lords as Baron Bergavenny. On his death, his wife Joan held the entire barony in survivorship for life until 1435, at which time it passed into the Nevill family; Joan's granddaughter Elizabeth, heir to the barony, married a Neville, [Edward Nevill, 3rd Baron Bergavenny](/wiki/Edward_Nevill,_3rd_Baron_Bergavenny "Edward Nevill, 3rd Baron Bergavenny").[[26]](#cite_note-26) From him it has descended continuously, the title being increased to an [earldom](/wiki/Earl "Earl") in 1784; and in 1876 [William Nevill](/wiki/William_Nevill,_1st_Marquess_of_Abergavenny "William Nevill, 1st Marquess of Abergavenny") [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] 5th Earl, an indefatigable and powerful supporter of the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), was created 1st [Marquess of Abergavenny](/wiki/Marquess_of_Abergavenny "Marquess of Abergavenny").[[17]](#cite_note-EB1911-17)

[Coldbrook Park](/wiki/Coldbrook_Park "Coldbrook Park") was a country house in an estate some 1+1⁄4 miles (2 km) southeast of the town. The house was originally built in the 14th century and belonged to the Herbert family for many generations until purchased by John Hanbury for his son, the diplomat Sir [Charles Hanbury Williams](/wiki/Charles_Hanbury_Williams "Charles Hanbury Williams").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Sir Charles reconstructed the house in 1746 with the addition of a nine-[bay](/wiki/Bay_window "Bay window") two-storey [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") [façade](/wiki/Fa%C3%A7ade "Façade") with a [Doric](/wiki/Doric_order "Doric order") [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico"). It was subsequently passed down in the Hanbury Williams family until it was demolished in 1954.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Events")]

Held during the first week of August every year, the [National Eisteddfod](/wiki/National_Eisteddfod "National Eisteddfod") is a celebration of the culture and language in Wales. The festival travels from place to place, alternating between north and south Wales, attracting around 150,000 visitors and over 250 tradestands and stalls. In 2016 [it was held in Abergavenny](/wiki/2016_Monmouthshire_and_District_National_Eisteddfod "2016 Monmouthshire and District National Eisteddfod") for the first time since 1913. The Chair and Crown for 2016 were presented to the festival's Executive Committee at a ceremony held in Monmouth on 14 June 2016.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The [Abergavenny Food Festival](/wiki/Abergavenny_Food_Festival "Abergavenny Food Festival") is held in the second week of September each year. The Steam, Veteran and Vintage Rally takes place in May every year. The event expands year on year with the 2016 rally including a rock choir, shire horses, motorcycling stunts, vintage cars and steam engines.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The Country and Western Music Festival is attended by enthusiasts of [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"). It marked its third year in 2016 and was attended by acts including Ben Thompson, LA Country and many more. The event was last held in 2017.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) The Abergavenny Writing Festival began in April 2016 and is a celebration of writing and the written word.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The Abergavenny Arts Festival, first held in 2018, celebrates arts in their broadest sense and showcases amateur and professional artists from the vibrant local arts scene together with some from further afield.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Welsh language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Welsh language")]

In recent decades, the number of Welsh speakers in the town has increased dramatically. The 2001 census recorded that 10% of the local population spoke the language, a five-fold increase over ten years from the figure of 2% recorded in 1991.[[35]](#cite_note-Welsh_Language_Board-35) The town has one of the two Welsh-medium primary schools in Monmouthshire, Ysgol Gymraeg y Fenni,[[36]](#cite_note-Ysgol_Gymraeg_y_Fenni-36) which was founded in the early 1990s. It is also home to the Abergavenny Welsh society, Cymreigyddion y Fenni,[[37]](#cite_note-Cymreigyddion_y_Fenni-37) and the local Abergavenny Eisteddfod.[[38]](#cite_note-Abergavenny_Eisteddfod-38)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Sport")]

Abergavenny was the home of [Abergavenny Thursdays F.C.](/wiki/Abergavenny_Thursdays_F.C. "Abergavenny Thursdays F.C."), formed in 1927 and merged with Govilon, the local village side in 2013. The new club, [Abergavenny Town F.C.](/wiki/Abergavenny_Town_F.C. "Abergavenny Town F.C."), plays at the Pen-y-pound Stadium, maintained and run by Thursday’s football trust, as members of the [Ardal South East](/wiki/Ardal_Leagues "Ardal Leagues") league (tier 3) for the 2021–22 season. It is also the home of [Abergavenny RFC](/wiki/Abergavenny_RFC "Abergavenny RFC"), a [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club founded in 1875 that plays at [Bailey Park, Abergavenny](/wiki/Bailey_Park,_Abergavenny "Bailey Park, Abergavenny"). In the 2018–19 season, they play in the [Welsh Rugby Union](/wiki/Welsh_Rugby_Union "Welsh Rugby Union") Division Three East A league.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Abergavenny [Hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") Club, formed in 1897, currently play at the Abergavenny Leisure Centre on Old Hereford Road.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Abergavenny Cricket Club play at [Pen-y-Pound](/wiki/Pen-y-Pound,_Abergavenny "Pen-y-Pound, Abergavenny"), Avenue Road and [Glamorgan CCC](/wiki/Glamorgan_County_Cricket_Club "Glamorgan County Cricket Club") also play some of their games here. Abergavenny Cricket Club was founded in 1834 and celebrated the 175th anniversary of its foundation in 2009.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Abergavenny Tennis Club also play at [Pen-y-Pound](/wiki/Pen-y-Pound,_Abergavenny "Pen-y-Pound, Abergavenny") and plays in the South Wales Doubles League and Aegon Team Tennis. The club engages the services of a head tennis professional to run a coaching programme for the town and was crowned [Tennis Wales](/wiki/Tennis_Wales "Tennis Wales")' Club of the Year in 2010.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Abergavenny hosted the [British National Cycling Championships](/wiki/British_National_Road_Race_Championships "British National Road Race Championships") in 2007, 2009 and 2014, as part of the town's Festival of Cycling.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Cattle market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Cattle market")]

A [cattle](/wiki/Cattle "Cattle") market was held in Abergavenny from 1863 to December 2013.[[4]](#cite_note-EB-4)[[44]](#cite_note-44) From 1825 to 1863 a [sheep](/wiki/Domestic_sheep "Domestic sheep") market was held at a site in Castle Street, to stop the sale of sheep on the streets of the town. When the market closed, the site was leased and operated by Abergavenny Market Auctioneers Ltd, who held regular livestock auctions on the site. Market days were held on Tuesdays for the auction sale of finished sheep, cull ewe/store and fodder (hay and straw), and on some Fridays for the auction sale of cattle. After [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales")'s cattle market closed in 2009 for redevelopment, Newport's sales were held at Abergavenny every Wednesday.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

In 2011 doubts about the future of Abergavenny Cattle Market were raised after [Monmouthshire County Council](/wiki/Monmouthshire_County_Council "Monmouthshire County Council") granted planning permission for its demolition and replacement with a supermarket, car park, and library.[[46]](#cite_note-46) In January 2012 the Welsh Government announced the repeal of the Abergavenny Improvement Acts of 1854 to 1871 which obliged the holding of a livestock market within the boundaries of Abergavenny town;[[47]](#cite_note-47) that repeal being effective from 26 March 2012.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The county council, which requested that the Abergavenny Improvement Acts be repealed, supported plans for a new cattle market to be established about 10 miles (16 km) from Abergavenny in countryside at Bryngwyn, some 3 miles (5 km) from [Raglan](/wiki/Raglan,_Monmouthshire "Raglan, Monmouthshire"). There was local opposition to this site.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) The new Monmouthshire Livestock Centre, a 27-acre site at Bryngwyn, opened in November 2013.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Cultural history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Cultural history")]

Abergavenny has hosted the [National Eisteddfod of Wales](/wiki/National_Eisteddfod_of_Wales "National Eisteddfod of Wales") in 1838, 1913 and most recently in 2016. In 2017 the town was named one of the best places to live in Wales.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) The town's local radio stations are currently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] [Sunshine Radio](/wiki/Sunshine_Radio_(Herefordshire_and_Monmouthshire) "Sunshine Radio (Herefordshire and Monmouthshire)") 107.8 FM and NH Sound 1287 AM. Abergavenny is home to an award-winning brass band.[[55]](#cite_note-55) Formed in Abergavenny prior to 1884,[[56]](#cite_note-56) the band were joint National Welsh League Champions in 2006[[57]](#cite_note-57) and joint National Welsh League Champions in 2011.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The band also operates a Junior Band training local young musicians.

The [Borough Theatre](/wiki/Borough_Theatre "Borough Theatre") in Abergavenny town centre hosts live events covering drama, opera, ballet, music, children's events, dance, comedy, storytelling, tribute bands and talks.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The Melville Centre is close to the town centre and includes the Melville Theatre, which hosts a range of live events.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The town held its first Abergavenny Arts Festival in 2018[[61]](#cite_note-61) and also hosts the [Abergavenny Food Festival](/wiki/Abergavenny_Food_Festival "Abergavenny Food Festival") in September each year.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare")'s play *[Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_(play) "Henry VIII (play)")* features the character Lord Abergavenny. In 1968 "Abergavenny" was the title of a UK single by [Marty Wilde](/wiki/Marty_Wilde "Marty Wilde"). In 1969, it was also released in the US, under a Marty Wilde pseudonym *Shannon*, where it was also a minor hit.[[63]](#cite_note-63) In [The Adventure of the Priory School](/wiki/The_Adventure_of_the_Priory_School "The Adventure of the Priory School") [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") refers to a case he is working on in Abergavenny.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Abergavenny is mentioned by Stan Shunpike, the conductor of the Knight Bus when the bus takes a detour there to drop off a passenger in [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling")'s *[Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Prisoner_of_Azkaban "Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban")*. The TV series *[Upstairs, Downstairs](/wiki/Upstairs,_Downstairs_(1971_TV_series) "Upstairs, Downstairs (1971 TV series)")*, features a character in the second season, [Thomas Watkins](/wiki/Thomas_Watkins "Thomas Watkins"), the devious Bellamy family [chauffeur](/wiki/Chauffeur "Chauffeur"), who comes from Abergavenny. In the 1979 spinoff of *Upstairs, Downstairs* titled *[Thomas & Sarah](/wiki/Thomas_%26_Sarah "Thomas & Sarah")*, Watkins and [Sarah Moffat](/wiki/Sarah_Moffat "Sarah Moffat"), another major character, marry and return briefly to Abergavenny. \* Much of the 1996 film, *[Intimate Relations](/wiki/Intimate_Relations_(1996_film) "Intimate Relations (1996 film)")* starring [Julie Walters](/wiki/Julie_Walters "Julie Walters"), [Rupert Graves](/wiki/Rupert_Graves "Rupert Graves"), [Les Dennis](/wiki/Les_Dennis "Les Dennis") and [Amanda Holden](/wiki/Amanda_Holden "Amanda Holden"), was filmed at many locations in and around Abergavenny.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Railway")]

[Abergavenny railway station](/wiki/Abergavenny_railway_station "Abergavenny railway station") lies on the [Welsh Marches Line](/wiki/Welsh_Marches_Line "Welsh Marches Line") from [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales") to [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford"). The weekday daytime service pattern typically sees one train per hour in each direction between [Manchester Piccadilly](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_station "Manchester Piccadilly station") and [Cardiff Central](/wiki/Cardiff_Central_railway_station "Cardiff Central railway station"), with most trains continuing beyond Cardiff to [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea_railway_station "Swansea railway station") and west Wales. There is also a two-hourly service between Cardiff and the [North Wales Coast Line](/wiki/North_Wales_Coast_Line "North Wales Coast Line") to [Holyhead](/wiki/Holyhead_railway_station "Holyhead railway station"), via [Wrexham General](/wiki/Wrexham_General_railway_station "Wrexham General railway station"). These services are all operated by [Transport for Wales](/wiki/Transport_for_Wales_Rail "Transport for Wales Rail").[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Roads")]

The town is located where the [A40 trunk road](/wiki/A40_road "A40 road") and the [A465 *Heads of the Valleys* road](/wiki/A465_road "A465 road") meet. The latter used to meet the A40 in the town centre but the A465 now runs to the east of the town centre.

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Buses")]

A network of services link the town with local villages. In addition, [Stagecoach South Wales](/wiki/Stagecoach_South_Wales "Stagecoach South Wales") operate service 23 to [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford") and Newport approximately every two hours while [Newport Bus](/wiki/Newport_Bus "Newport Bus") operates service 83 to [Monmouth](/wiki/Monmouth "Monmouth").

## Notable buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notable buildings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Grade_II_listed_Church_of_the_Holy_Trinity%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2723503.jpg/250px-Grade_II_listed_Church_of_the_Holy_Trinity%2C_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2723503.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grade_II_listed_Church_of_the_Holy_Trinity,_Abergavenny_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2723503.jpg)

Church of the Holy Trinity

[Abergavenny Castle](/wiki/Abergavenny_Castle "Abergavenny Castle") is located strategically just south of the town centre overlooking the [River Usk](/wiki/River_Usk "River Usk"). It was built in about 1067 by the Norman baron [Hamelin de Ballon](/wiki/Hamelin_de_Ballon "Hamelin de Ballon") to guard against incursions by the Welsh from the hills to the north and west. All that remains is defensive ditches and the ruins of the stone keep, towers, and part of the curtain wall. It is a [Grade I listed building](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Abergavenny_Butter_Market_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1313202.jpg/250px-Abergavenny_Butter_Market_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1313202.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abergavenny_Butter_Market_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1313202.jpg)

Abergavenny Market Hall in 2009

Various markets are held in the Market Hall, for example: Tuesdays, Fridays and Saturdays – retail market; Wednesdays – flea market; fourth Thursday of each month – [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market"); third Sunday of each month – antique fair; second Saturday of each month – craft fair.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") church of the Holy Trinity is in the [Diocese of Monmouth](/wiki/Diocese_of_Monmouth "Diocese of Monmouth"). Holy Trinity Church was consecrated by the Bishop of Llandaff on 6 November 1840. It was originally built as a chapel to serve the adjacent almshouses and the nearby school. It has been Grade II listed since January 1974.

Other listed buildings in the town include the parish [Priory Church of St Mary](/wiki/Priory_Church_of_St_Mary,_Abergavenny "Priory Church of St Mary, Abergavenny"), a medieval and Victorian building that was originally the church of the Benedictine priory founded in Abergavenny before 1100; the sixteenth century [Tithe Barn near St Mary's](/wiki/The_Tithe_Barn,_Abergavenny "The Tithe Barn, Abergavenny"); the Victorian Church of the Holy Trinity; the Grade II\* listed [St John's Masonic Lodge](/wiki/St_John%27s_Church,_Abergavenny "St John's Church, Abergavenny"); [Abergavenny Museum](/wiki/Abergavenny_Museum "Abergavenny Museum"); the Public Library; the [Town Hall](/wiki/Abergavenny_Town_Hall "Abergavenny Town Hall"); and the remains of [Abergavenny town walls](/wiki/Abergavenny_town_walls "Abergavenny town walls") behind Neville Street.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

From 1851, the Monmouthshire lunatic asylum, later [Pen-y-Fal Hospital](/wiki/Pen-y-Fal_Hospital "Pen-y-Fal Hospital"), a [psychiatric hospital](/wiki/Psychiatric_hospital "Psychiatric hospital"), stood on the outskirts of Abergavenny. Between 1851 and 1950, over 3,000 patients died at the hospital. A memorial plaque for the deceased has now been placed at the site. After its closure in the 1990s, its buildings and grounds were redeveloped as housing.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Some psychiatric services are now administered from [Maindiff Court Hospital](/wiki/Maindiff_Court_Hospital "Maindiff Court Hospital") on the outskirts of the town, close to the foot of the [Skirrid](/wiki/Skirrid "Skirrid") mountain.

## Parks and gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Parks and gardens")]

Abergavenny has three public [urban parks](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") which are listed on the [Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales](/wiki/Cadw/ICOMOS_Register_of_Parks_and_Gardens_of_Special_Historic_Interest_in_Wales "Cadw/ICOMOS Register of Parks and Gardens of Special Historic Interest in Wales"): the grounds of [Abergavenny Castle](/wiki/Abergavenny_Castle "Abergavenny Castle"),[[70]](#cite_note-70) [Linda Vista Gardens](/wiki/Linda_Vista_Gardens "Linda Vista Gardens")[[71]](#cite_note-71) and [Bailey Park](/wiki/Bailey_Park,_Abergavenny "Bailey Park, Abergavenny").[[72]](#cite_note-72) A fourth registered garden, at The Hill to the north of the town, forms part of the grounds of a residential development.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

* [Beaupréau](/wiki/Beaupr%C3%A9au "Beaupréau"), France[[74]](#cite_note-twin-74)
* [Sarno](/wiki/Sarno "Sarno"), Italy

## Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Military")]

One of the eleven [Victoria Cross medals](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") won at [Rorke's Drift](/wiki/Rorke%27s_Drift "Rorke's Drift") was awarded to John Fielding from Abergavenny. He had enlisted under the false name of [Williams](/wiki/John_Williams_(VC) "John Williams (VC)"). One was also awarded for the same action to [Robert Jones](/wiki/Robert_Jones_(VC) "Robert Jones (VC)"), born at Clytha between Abergavenny and [Raglan](/wiki/Raglan,_Monmouthshire "Raglan, Monmouthshire"). Another Abergavenny-born soldier, [Thomas Monaghan](/wiki/Thomas_Monaghan_(VC) "Thomas Monaghan (VC)") received his VC for defending his colonel during the [Indian Rebellion](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion "Indian Rebellion"). In 1908 following the formation of the [Territorial Force](/wiki/Territorial_Force "Territorial Force") the Abergavenny Cadet Corps was formed and affiliated with the 3rd Battalion, [The Monmouthshire Regiment](/wiki/The_Monmouthshire_Regiment "The Monmouthshire Regiment"). In 1912 the regiment was affiliated with the new formed 1st Cadet Battalion, [The Monmouthshire Regiment](/wiki/The_Monmouthshire_Regiment "The Monmouthshire Regiment").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Abergavenny&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Notable people")]

*See also [Category:People from Abergavenny](/wiki/Category:People_from_Abergavenny "Category:People from Abergavenny")*

* [Augustine Baker](/wiki/Augustine_Baker "Augustine Baker") (1575–1641), well-known Benedictine mystic and an ascetic writer. He was one of the earliest members of the English Benedictine Congregation which was newly restored to England after the Reformation.
* [John Williams](/wiki/John_Williams_(VC) "John Williams (VC)") (1857–1932) soldier, recipient of the [Victoria Cross](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") for actions at [Rorke’s Drift](/wiki/Battle_of_Rorke%27s_Drift "Battle of Rorke's Drift").
* [Scott Ellaway](/wiki/Scott_Ellaway "Scott Ellaway") (born 1981), conductor, was born and brought up locally.
* [Becky James](/wiki/Becky_James "Becky James") (born 1991), racing cyclist, double gold medallist at the [2013 UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/2013_UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "2013 UCI Track Cycling World Championships") and double silver medallist at the [2016 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Cycling at the 2016 Summer Olympics"), was born and grew up in Abergavenny.
* [Matthew Jay](/wiki/Matthew_Jay "Matthew Jay") (1978–2003), singer-songwriter, spent much of his life in the town.
* [Peter Law](/wiki/Peter_Law "Peter Law") (1948–2006), politician and Independent MP, notable for defeating the Labour candidate in the safest Welsh seat during the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election") was born in Abergavenny.[[76]](#cite_note-76)
* Saint [David Lewis](/wiki/David_Lewis_(Jesuit_priest) "David Lewis (Jesuit priest)") (1616–1679), [Catholic priest](/wiki/Priesthood_(Catholic_Church) "Priesthood (Catholic Church)") and [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr"), was born in Abergavenny and prayed in the local [Gunter Mansion](/wiki/Gunter_Mansion "Gunter Mansion").
* [Malcolm Nash](/wiki/Malcolm_Nash "Malcolm Nash") (1945–2019), cricketer, famous for bowling to [Gary Sobers](/wiki/Garfield_Sobers "Garfield Sobers") who hit six sixes in one Nash over, was born in Abergavenny.
* [Mary Penry](/wiki/Mary_Penry "Mary Penry") (1735–1804), Moravian sister in 18th-century [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") was born in Abergavenny.
* [Owen Sheers](/wiki/Owen_Sheers "Owen Sheers") (born 1974), poet, grew up in Abergavenny.
* [Oliver Thornton](/wiki/Oliver_Thornton "Oliver Thornton") (born 1979), West End actor, starred of *[Priscilla, Queen of the Desert](/wiki/Priscilla,_Queen_of_the_Desert_(musical) "Priscilla, Queen of the Desert (musical)")*, was born and grew up in Abergavenny.
* [Vulcana](/wiki/Vulcana "Vulcana") (Miriam Kate Williams, 1874–1946), world-famous strongwoman, was born in Abergavenny.
* [Ethel Lina White](/wiki/Ethel_Lina_White "Ethel Lina White") (1876–1944), crime writer best known for her novel *[The Wheel Spins](/wiki/The_Wheel_Spins "The Wheel Spins")* (1936), on which the Alfred Hitchcock film *[The Lady Vanishes](/wiki/The_Lady_Vanishes "The Lady Vanishes")* (1938) was based.
* [Jules Williams](/wiki/Jules_Williams "Jules Williams") (born 1968), writer, director, and producer of *The Weigh Forward*.[[77]](#cite_note-77)
* [Raymond Williams](/wiki/Raymond_Williams "Raymond Williams"), (1921–1988) academic, critic and writer was born and brought up locally.
* [Dave Richards](/wiki/Dave_Richards_(footballer,_born_1993) "Dave Richards (footballer, born 1993)"), (1993) professional footballer for [Crewe Alexandra](/wiki/Crewe_Alexandra "Crewe Alexandra") was born and raised in the town.
* [Marina Diamandis](/wiki/Marina_Diamandis "Marina Diamandis") (1985), professional singer and songwriter
